sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Crush injury occurs when a body part is subjected to a high degree of force or pressure, usually after being squeezed between two heavy objects. This can result in damage to skin, muscle, nerves, blood vessels, and bones. Crush injuries include simple mechanical crush injury, compartment syndrome, and crush syndrome. There are many causes ranging from isolated limb injuries, multisystem trauma, envenomation, drug and toxin exposure, heat stroke, burns and some bacteria/infections. Left untreated, these can lead to tissue necrosis, permanent impairment and crush syndrome.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Compartment syndrome is characterised by:**
   - Palpable tension of swelling of an anatomical compartment
   - Pain disproportionate to the injury
   - Pain on passive stretching of muscles within the anatomical compartment
   - Paraesthesia of skin and paresis of muscles supplied by nerves traversing the compartment
   - Pallor of skin over the compartment
   - Pulses diminishing as the condition develops is common, but normal peripheral pulses and capillary refill is not uncommon

**Crush syndrome is characterised by:**
   - Compartment syndrome
   - Haemodynamic instability
   - Reperfusion injuries leading to:
   - Lactic acidosis and hyperkalaemia
   - Dysrhythmias
   - Myoglobinuria (dark “tea” coloured urine) leading to renal failure
   - Shock
   - Hypothermia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Prolonged compression, large muscle groups, and delayed release increase the risk of crush syndrome, hyperkalaemia, shock, and acute kidney injury
- Be prepared for deterioration immediately after release of the crushing force
- Compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency
- Tourniquets are for life-threatening bleeding, not routine prevention of reperfusion injury

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety and request additional rescue resources early
- Control catastrophic bleeding and be prepared for major bleeding on release
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration where indicated
- Administer oxygen if indicated and record vital signs and rhythm
- Minimise further tissue injury, manage temperature, and monitor for tense swollen compartments and pain out of proportion
- Seek early senior consultation before release when prolonged entrapment is present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV or IO access, ideally before release if compression has been prolonged
- Begin IV fluids early when clinically indicated to reduce the impact of reperfusion injury; target urine output of at least 1 mL/kg/hour to reduce the risk of myoglobin-related acute kidney injury - monitor urine colour and output where possible
- Provide analgesia and antiemetic therapy within scope
- Monitor closely for hyperkalaemia, dysrhythmia, shock, and compartment syndrome
- Treat hyperkalaemia under the relevant guideline if it develops
- **Fluid resuscitation target in crush injury / rhabdomyolysis:** Urine output ≥1 mL/kg/hr (to protect against acute kidney injury from myoglobin precipitation in renal tubules). This is the primary monitoring target guiding ongoing fluid administration - aggressive early hydration is the cornerstone of AKI prevention.
- Monitor ECG for hyperkalaemia changes: peaked T-waves, widened QRS, sine-wave pattern, absent P-waves - hyperkalaemia may develop rapidly and can be life-threatening

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - A person with a crush injury may not complain of pain and there may be no external signs of injury. Any person who has been subjected to crush injury, including from their own body weight, should be taken to hospital for further review and management
